content.summary_short: Learn discovery questions that reveal the actual need instead of chasing the first surface request.
content.summary_long: This episode teaches commercial discovery: problem, outcome, timeline, readiness, and the difference between the surface request and the deeper need that should guide routing.
content.start_here: Use the listen pass to hear the discovery mindset, then use the slides to practice wants-versus-needs thinking.
content.key_line: Do not pitch too early. Find the problem shape first.

slides.title: Do not pitch too early
slides.goal: Slow the learner down at the right moment.
bullets: Early pitching often solves the wrong problem.
A surface request may be inaccurate, incomplete, or too narrow.
Discovery protects both routing quality and trust.

slides.title: The discovery rule
bullets: Start with the problem, desired outcome, and timeline.
Those three anchors tell you much more than a feature request alone.
Until those are clearer, recommendations stay provisional.

slides.title: Wants versus needs
bullets: A lead may ask for a website when the deeper need is lead flow or trust repair.
A lead may ask for content when the deeper need is a learning path or sales clarity.
Surface wants matter, but they are not always the primary route signal.
slides.takeaway: The first request is often a clue, not the full answer.

slides.title: Questions that find truth
bullets: What problem are you trying to solve?
What changes after success, and what timeline matters?
What have you already tried, and who is affected if nothing changes?
slides.takeaway: Ask for the problem, the outcome, and the cost of inaction.

slides.title: Readiness signals
slides.goal: Separate ready leads from blurry ones.
bullets: Some leads are ready now. Others need more clarity. Some are not ready at all.
Readiness changes the next step you recommend.
Pushing a not-ready lead into a fake solution is still a sales failure.

slides.title: Primary lane versus support lane
bullets: A lead can have one primary need and still benefit from support lanes.
The primary lane should address the core problem, not just the nicest add-on.
Support lanes are real, but they should not hide the core route.

slides.title: Worked discovery example
slides.goal: Turn the framework into a real scenario.
bullets: Surface request: We need a website refresh. Real issue after discovery: the owner has weak follow-up, no clear offer, and no lead intake automation.
Better qualification summary: primary need is commercial clarity plus lead-flow structure, so Lumina + Nexus is more plausible than a design-only refresh.
Good discovery changed the route because it exposed the real business problem, not just the front-end symptom.
slides.takeaway: Discovery should rewrite the problem statement before you recommend the lane.

slides.title: Write the qualification summary
slides.goal: Turn conversation into usable routing notes.
bullets: Capture the real problem, desired outcome, likely primary division, readiness, and bundle signals.
That summary becomes the bridge into recommendation and handoff.
If the summary is muddy, the next step will be muddy too.

references.blurb: Use the original lane docs plus ByteCast standards to reinforce this episode.

advance.label: EP-LUM-105
advance.description: Complete EP-LUM-104 gates, then continue into Recommend the Right Path.

quest.title: EP-LUM-104 practice checklist
quest.prompt: Use the checklist to train discovery discipline before you recommend a path.

items.text: Ask the discovery rule aloud: problem, outcome, timeline.
Turn one surface request into a deeper need statement.
Name one signal that a lead may not be sales-ready yet.
Write one sentence describing the primary lane and one possible support lane for a sample lead.

quiz.prompt: Pass the discovery check before you move into recommendation logic.

choices: Ask about problem, outcome, and timeline
questions.explain: That frame reveals the real need.

questions.q: A lead asks for a website, but the real issue is lead flow automation. The website request is:
questions.explain: The first request is not always the core need.

choices: They change the next step and recommendation quality
They remove the need for notes
questions.explain: Readiness affects how you move the opportunity forward.

questions.q: How should support lanes be used?
choices: To support the primary route without replacing it
questions.explain: Support lanes help, but the primary need still leads.

questions.q: In the worked website-refresh example, what changed after discovery?
choices: The lead no longer needed help
The issue became clearer as commercial clarity plus lead-flow structure
The safest route became Horizon only
questions.explain: Discovery exposed the deeper commercial and systems need behind the website request.
